concepts.title: Vectors, matrices & the operations that matter

beats.text: A vector is an ordered list of numbers, seen at once as an arrow from the origin and as a single data point.

visual.sub: An arrow, a data point, and a function

beats.text: A matrix wears two hats: a table where rows are examples and columns are features, and a function mapping vectors to vectors.
The key re-reading is that a matrix times a vector is a linear combination of the columns weighted by the vector's entries.
This is exactly what a fully-connected layer computes when it evaluates y equals W x plus b a few billion times a second.
Multiplication is associative and distributive but not commutative: rotating then stretching differs from stretching then rotating.
Half of all numerical bugs are shape bugs, so for a 2 by 3 times a 3 by 4 the result has 8 entries.
To do geometry you need length and angle, and the dot product supplies both at once.
The dot product equals the product of lengths times the cosine of the angle, and the L2 norm is the square root of the self dot product.
Take the vector three four: its L2 norm is the square root of nine plus sixteen, which is five, the classic 3-4-5 triangle.
The same vector has L1 norm seven and max norm four, so the max norm never exceeds L2, which never exceeds L1.
Cosine similarity is the dot product after normalizing length, the default way to compare embeddings since it cares about direction.
Projection finds the closest point on a line, and generalized to a subspace this idea is exactly least-squares regression.

concepts.title: A matrix as a linear map; rank & null space

beats.text: A matrix is a linear map: it sends lines to lines, keeps the origin fixed, and respects linear combinations.

visual.sub: What a matrix can and cannot do

beats.text: A 2 by 2 matrix can rotate, scale, shear, reflect, or flatten the plane, and nothing else.
The column space is all outputs and the null space is the inputs crushed to zero; rank counts the genuinely independent output directions.
Every input dimension either survives into the output or is annihilated, so rank plus nullity equals the number of columns.
The determinant ad minus bc is the area-scaling factor, and a zero determinant flattens the plane and drops the rank.
Real data matrices are nearly low-rank: a thousand correlated survey columns might carry effective rank around twenty.
Most directions get rotated by a matrix, but a precious few are only stretched, leaving their line untouched.

visual.sub: The directions a map never turns

beats.text: The eigenvalue equation says applying A to v just rescales it by lambda, which requires A minus lambda I to be singular.
For the matrix with diagonal 2 and 2 and off-diagonal 1, trace is four, determinant is three, and the eigenvalues are 3 and 1.
Eigenvalues sum to the trace and multiply to the determinant, giving a free correctness check on any computation.
PCA diagonalizes the covariance matrix and PageRank is the dominant eigenvector of the web's link matrix.
A real matrix can have complex eigenvalues; a pure rotation has eigenvalues plus and minus i with no real eigenvector.

concepts.title: The Singular Value Decomposition — the master factorization

beats.text: Eigen-decomposition is fussy, but the singular value decomposition factors every matrix as a rotation, a scaling, and another rotation.
Any matrix equals U Sigma V transpose, with orthonormal U and V and singular values sorted in decreasing order on the diagonal.
Keeping the largest k singular values gives the best possible rank-k approximation, the Eckart-Young theorem.
The reconstruction error is governed entirely by the singular values you threw away, so a fast-decaying spectrum compresses well.
On a 16 by 16 grid a rank-3 factorization stores 96 numbers instead of 256, and the gap widens enormously at scale.
The connection to PCA is exact: center the data, take its SVD, and the right singular vectors are the principal components.
